description: §19.7.3 explicit `match` over a failable (`!`) result — the implicit `::Ok(v)` success arm. computeOk: safeDiv(10,2) succeeds and returns 5 BARE (un-tagged); the `::Ok(v)` arm fires and binds v = the whole success value, so @okOut = 'ok: 5'. computeErr: safeDiv(10,0) fails DivErr.DivByZero (a { __scrml_error,... } envelope); the `::DivByZero` arm routes and @errOut = 'ERR: divide by zero'. Proves the success arm both FIRES and BINDS, and the error arm still routes by .variant (GitHub #21).
rationale: §19.7 / §19.5.2: a `!` function returns its success value BARE and its error path as a tagged `{ __scrml_error, type, variant, data }` envelope (§19.9.1). The `::Ok` variant is the implicit success wrapper (§19.7.3) — `match` must discriminate success (bare value → Ok(value)) from error (envelope → its .variant). Before the fix the tag discriminator read `.variant` off the bare success (never 'Ok', so `::Ok` never fired) and left `v` unbound. E-ERROR-* silent (safeDiv is `!`, uses `fail` legally; both matches handle the result).
